mappings.rationaleAI: Ancient Greece addresses archaic (B.1), classical (B.2), and Hellenistic (C) periods.
The Roman Republic addresses Rome's rise (D), early Rome (D.2), early (D.3), middle (D.4), and late (D.5) Republic.
The Roman Empire addresses Julio-Claudians (E.1), Flavians/Antonines (E.2), and zenith (E.3).
Alexander addresses his empire (B.2.h).
Hellenistic Age addresses kingdoms (C.1), interstate relations (C.2), and culture (C.3).
Etruscans address A.3.a.i directly.
Thucydides addresses the Peloponnesian War (B.2.c).
Herodotus addresses Greco-Persian Wars (B.1.c.viii).
Homer relates to the Geometric Period (B.1.b).
Classical literature relates to 4th-century Greek culture (B.2.g), Augustan culture (E.1.d), and Greco-Roman culture (E.3.b).
Celts address A.3.b.i and Celtic migrations (C.2.c).
Spartans address Spartan dominance (B.1.c.ii) and hegemony (B.2.d).
Ancient warfare relates to the Peloponnesian War (B.2.c), Punic Wars (D.4.a), and imperial expansion (E.1).
Ancient science relates to Archaic philosophy (B.1.c.vii), 4th-century culture (B.2.g), and Greco-Roman culture (E.3.b).
Late Antiquity relates to 3rd-4th century changes (E.4).
Byzantium relates to Constantine's Constantinople (E.4.e.ii).
Christianity relates to its spread (E.4.b) and state religion establishment (E.4.f.iii).
Horace relates to Augustan culture (E.1.d).
Ovid relates to Augustan culture (E.1.d).
Cicero relates to mid-1st century politics (D.5.d.ii) and late republican culture (D.5.h).
Socrates relates to intellectual changes during the Peloponnesian War (B.2.c).
Aristotle relates to 4th-century Greek culture (B.2.g).
Classics relates broadly to Greece (B), Rome (D), and the empire (E).
Ancient philosophy relates to beginnings of philosophy (B.1.c.vii), 4th-century culture (B.2.g), and Hellenistic philosophy (C.3.b).
Stoicism relates to Hellenistic philosophy (C.3.b) and Greco-Roman culture (E.3.b).
Epicureanism relates to Hellenistic philosophy (C.3.b).
Paganism relates to trans-Alpine Europeans (A.3.b) and imperial religions (E.4.b).
Druids relate to the Celts (A.3.b.i).
Roman Britain relates to later empire provinces (E.4.g).
Democracy relates to Cleisthenes (B.1.c.ii) and Periclean Athens (B.2.a).
Sophocles relates to Athenian cultural efflorescence (B.2.a.iii).
Epic relates to Homer and Hesiod (B.1.b).
Mythology relates to Geometric Period religion (B.1.b).
Rhetoric relates to late republican oratory (D.5.h).

Covers the spread of Neolithic farming communities and Bronze Age developments across Europe, directly addressing the geography, ethnography, and archaeological sources of non-Classical ancient Europe including population movements and the Indo-Europeans.

Examines the Late Bronze Age eastern Mediterranean context including Homer's Iliad, Hittite interactions with Anatolia, and the Sea Peoples phenomenon, directly relevant to the early Archaic period and the Dorian invasions that followed the Bronze Age collapse.

Covers Plato's philosophy addressing knowledge, ethics, metaphysics, and political thought in 5th-4th century BC Athens, directly relevant to Greek culture in the 4th century BC and developments in philosophy.

Covers the earliest Greek thinkers from Thales to Protagoras exploring the nature of reality, directly relevant to rationalism, irrationalism, and the beginnings of philosophy and science in the Archaic Period.
